title: Large file uploads (>~1MB) are silently corrupted — size-dependent, regression from ~1 week ago
problem: PDF files uploaded to task attachments are corrupted and cannot be opened. The corruption is size-dependent: small files (a few hundred KB) upload successfully, while larger files (3-4MB+) are damaged. This began approximately one week ago and affects multiple customers. Previously uploaded files remain intact.
root_cause_hypothesis: A recent change (~1 week ago) to the file upload pipeline is truncating or mangling files above a size threshold. Most likely candidates: (1) a request body size limit was reduced (e.g., nginx/reverse proxy max_body_size, API gateway limit), (2) chunked transfer encoding was introduced or broken, (3) a streaming/buffering change is cutting off uploads mid-transfer, or (4) a base64 or multipart encoding change is corrupting the byte stream for payloads above a certain size.
reproduction_steps: Upload a small PDF (<500KB) to a task attachment — confirm it opens correctly
Upload a larger PDF (3-5MB) to a task attachment
Download the larger PDF and attempt to open it — expect 'file is damaged' error
Compare the byte size of the original and uploaded file to determine if truncation occurred
Check hex dump of the corrupted file: does it end abruptly (truncation) or contain garbled bytes (encoding corruption)?
environment: Production environment, started approximately one week ago. Specific browser/OS not confirmed but reported across multiple customers, suggesting server-side issue.
impact: Multiple customers are affected. File attachments are a core workflow feature and corrupted files are visible to end-users, causing customer-facing reputation damage. Small files still work, providing a partial (but impractical) workaround.
recommended_fix: 1. Review all deployments and config changes from the past 7-10 days that touch file upload, storage, or proxy configuration. 2. Check reverse proxy / API gateway settings for request body size limits (e.g., nginx client_max_body_size, AWS API Gateway payload limit). 3. Check for changes to multipart parsing, streaming, or chunked transfer encoding in the upload handler. 4. Upload a known-good 5MB PDF in a staging environment and compare the stored bytes against the original (diff at byte level) to determine whether this is truncation or encoding corruption. 5. If truncation, the file size delta will reveal the exact cutoff point, which likely maps to a config value.
proposed_test_case: Upload PDFs of varying sizes (100KB, 1MB, 2MB, 5MB, 10MB) and verify that each downloaded file is byte-identical to the original. This should be an automated integration test against the upload endpoint that asserts Content-Length and SHA-256 hash match between source and stored file.
information_gaps: Exact error message from the PDF viewer (truncation vs. encoding clue — but developer can determine this from byte comparison)
Whether non-PDF file types are also affected (reporter only uses PDFs regularly)
Exact date the issue started and corresponding deployment
Whether the corruption manifests differently across browsers or upload methods
